settings.title: Inside the Cold Room Before the Doors Open
settings.excerpt: Our florist Nadia conditions every stem by hand at dawn so nothing reaches you tired.
settings.body:
The flowers in our window look effortless, but getting them there starts in the cold room well before opening.

Nadia, who has arranged for us since the first month, conditions every delivery by hand. That means stripping lower leaves, cutting each stem, and letting them drink deep in cool water for hours.

What conditioning actually does

Conditioning is the quiet work that decides whether a stem lasts a week or droops in two days. Stripping the lower leaves keeps them out of the water, where they would otherwise rot and breed the bacteria that clog a stem. A fresh cut opens the channels the flower drinks through, and the long, cool soak lets each one rehydrate fully after its journey to us. Skip any of it and the flower reaches you already tired, no matter how fresh it looked on the truck.

The cold room itself matters too. Flowers age fastest in warmth, so keeping them cool until the doors open buys days of vase life that you take home without ever knowing it.

Nadia's morning steps

  • Strip the lower leaves so nothing sits below the waterline.
  • Recut every stem to open it up for drinking.
  • Let them rest in cool, deep water for hours before they go out front.

Only then do they go out front. She says flowers are like people first thing in the morning. Give them water and a little quiet, and they come around.
